title: Folded envelopes in journals
subtitle: Attaching folded paper pockets and envelopes inside a journal for loose papers and ephemera
excerpt: A folded envelope glued or sewn into a journal page gives a pocket for loose items: tickets, photographs, folded notes, pressed flowers. Three constructions are covered: a simple diagonal-fold pocket, a squared-base envelope, and a side-seam pocket glued directly to the page.
sourceNotes: Paper folding for pockets and envelopes is a practical craft technique with a long history in both origami tradition and postal correspondence. The three constructions here are a synthesis of standard paper-folding and adhesive-attachment methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: In a glued pocket or envelope, the folded-over edges that form the seam. Turn-ins are glued flat against the back or side of the pocket to seal the seam.
A journal page or insert that has a folded or glued pocket attached, creating a concealed storage space for loose items.

content.text: keeps loose items with the journal entry they belong to rather than in a separate pile. The three methods here range from a 5-minute fold with no cutting to a more finished sewn pocket. All use paper already to hand: a page from a previous journal, a sheet of decorative paper, or even a used envelope. The
are the key to a tidy seam: they fold the raw edge under before gluing, so no cut edge is visible from the front.

items.name: Paper for the pocket (any paper 70 to 160 gsm, cut slightly smaller than the journal page)
Metal ruler and craft knife (for trimming to size, optional)

content.text: Method 1: diagonal-fold pocket (5 minutes, no cutting)
Take a square sheet of paper. Fold it diagonally corner to corner, crease firmly with the bone folder, and open.
Fold the bottom corner up to meet the centre crease line, leaving the top triangle open as the pocket opening. Crease.

Fold the left and right corners in to meet at the centre, slightly overlapping. These form the side seams. Apply PVA to the back of both side turn-ins and press down. Allow to dry before gluing the whole pocket into the journal page.

Cut a rectangle of paper: 1.5 times as tall as the intended pocket opening, and the same width as the journal page minus 10 mm each side. Score and fold the bottom third up to form the pocket body. Score and fold the top third down to form the flap.
Fold the left and right edges of the pocket body in by 8 mm. Apply PVA to these turn-ins and press them to the back of the flap section above. This seals the sides.
Apply PVA to the back of the finished envelope (the back of the pocket body) and glue onto the journal page. Tuck the flap into the pocket opening once dry.
Method 3: side-seam pocket directly on the page

Cut a rectangle of paper the full width of the journal page and approximately two-thirds the page height. Fold up the bottom third to form the pocket, leaving the top open. Apply PVA to the two short side edges and to the bottom fold, then press the entire assembly onto the journal page. Weight until dry. This creates a flush, neat pocket with the page as the pocket back.
